refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Cross-check with network logs, telemetry timestamps, and operator incident reports.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report link degradation or loss within [X] seconds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to achieve ≥0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication path with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover and link health monitoring
DP-C: Multi-carrier aggregation (cellular + satellite + mesh) with intelligent routing and seamless handover
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary dual-path (cellular + private network) with satellite backup and predictive link management
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid approach—primary dual-path (cellular + private network) with satellite backup, automatic failover logic, continuous link health monitoring, and predictive degradation detection to achieve ≥0.999 availability.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure selection (cellular, private, satellite providers)—this is acceptable because network choice is a design decision, not a functional requirement.
DP-D couples to Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface) for link-loss notifications—this is a controlled, intentional coupling that supports safety criticality.
DP-D couples to Vehicle (Tasking interface) for command transmission—this is a necessary functional coupling.
coupling_check.notes: No unintended or problematic couplings detected. The design is decoupled from specific operator behavior or site infrastructure beyond network availability assumptions.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Cellular and private network coverage is available at the worksite; if not, satellite backup alone may not meet 0.999 target.
Assumption: Failover switching time is <100 ms to avoid operator-perceived interruption.
Risk: Satellite latency (typically 500–1000 ms) may be unacceptable for real-time teleoperation; requires operator training or autonomous safety fallback.
Risk: Multi-carrier coordination adds complexity; synchronization failures could reduce availability.
Risk: Cost and regulatory approval for satellite uplink may exceed project constraints.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) failover switching time budget, (2) latency tolerance for each communication path, (3) link health monitoring interval and thresholds, (4) fallback behavior when all paths degrade, and (5) cost/regulatory feasibility gate before detailed design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the end-to-end teleoperation link availability solution, including dual-path communication logic, failover controller, link health monitoring, and integration with Safety Services for alert propagation. Responsible for achieving ≥0.999 availability through redundancy, failover, and predictive link management.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends operator commands (steering, speed, tool control) to Vehicle via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state, sensor data, and link health metrics from Vehicle via Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends link degradation/loss alerts to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface to trigger operator notifications and autonomous safety actions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module manages dual-path communication (cellular + private + satellite) through Network Link interface; monitors link state and executes failover logic.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover control, and health monitoring. All core DP-D functions (dual-path management, failover, monitoring) are contained within this module. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface is external (managed by infrastructure), and SafetyAlert coupling is intentional and well-defined.

Vehicle module owns the physical communication hardware (antennas, modems) but not the link availability logic or failover strategy. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter link management across vehicle and ground systems, violating cohesion.
Safety Services owns alert generation and safety policy enforcement, not communication infrastructure. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate safety response with link management.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained for detailed DP realization. Assigning DP-D here would defer architectural clarity to lower levels and obscure ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because it is functionally responsible for maintaining the operator-to-vehicle communication link, logically owns the failover and redundancy strategy, and has clear interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link infrastructure.
containment_risks: Risk: If Network Link interface is not fully abstracted, Teleoperation module may become tightly coupled to specific network technologies (cellular, satellite), reducing portability.
Risk: If Vehicle module does not expose link health metrics via Telemetry interface, Teleoperation cannot implement predictive monitoring.
Risk: If Safety Services does not respond promptly to SafetyAlert, link loss may not trigger safe shutdown in time.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that: (1) Network Link interface provides a unified abstraction for multi-carrier failover (not technology-specific), (2) Vehicle exposes link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, packet loss) via Telemetry, (3) Safety Services defines response time SLA for SafetyAlert, and (4) Teleoperation module includes a detailed failover state machine and health monitoring algorithm in design documentation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid dual-path + satellite + failover + monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and technically feasible. No functional gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Network Link interface is external; Teleoperation depends on infrastructure availability. Mitigation: Define Network Link SLA and fallback behavior in interface specification.

Diffusion risk: Vehicle module must expose link health metrics; if not available, Teleoperation cannot implement predictive monitoring. Mitigation: Confirm Telemetry interface includes link quality fields.
Diffusion risk: Safety Services must respond to SafetyAlert within acceptable time; slow response could negate link redundancy benefits. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert response time SLA.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. Core DP logic (failover, redundancy, monitoring) is fully contained. External dependencies (Network Link, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are well-defined interfaces with clear ownership boundaries. No unintended diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: link availability can be measured continuously during evaluation period using network monitoring tools, telemetry logs, and operator incident reports. Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is quantitative and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns link availability; Vehicle owns communication hardware; Safety Services owns alert response; Network Link infrastructure provides multi-carrier connectivity. Responsibilities are non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Refine Network Link interface specification to define failover switching time budget (<100 ms recommended) and multi-carrier abstraction layer.
Confirm Vehicle Telemetry interface includes link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, packet loss, carrier type).
Define SafetyAlert response time SLA (e.g., operator notification within 500 ms of link loss).
Document Teleoperation module failover state machine and health monitoring algorithm in design specification.
Establish evaluation period duration and measurement methodology (continuous monitoring vs. periodic sampling).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command uplink, telemetry downlink, link degradation detection, redundancy/failover). DP-D is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module, but further decomposition is required to detail the failover logic, health monitoring algorithm, and multi-carrier coordination. Proceeding to next level will clarify implementation details and reduce technical risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with <[X] ms latency.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with <[Y] ms latency.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation (RSSI drop, latency increase, packet loss) and report to Safety Services within [Z] seconds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall automatically failover from primary to backup communication path within [W] ms upon link loss, without operator intervention.

assumptions: Cellular and private network coverage is available at the worksite.
Satellite backup is available as tertiary path (cost and regulatory approval pending).
Failover switching time <100 ms is acceptable for operator experience.
Vehicle module exposes link health metrics via Telemetry interface.
Safety Services responds to SafetyAlert within 500 ms.
Evaluation period is ≥30 days of continuous operation to achieve statistical confidence in 0.999 availability.
open_risks: Satellite latency (500–1000 ms) may be unacceptable for real-time teleoperation; requires operator training or autonomous fallback.
Multi-carrier synchronization complexity could introduce race conditions or failover delays.
Cost and regulatory approval for satellite uplink may exceed project budget.
Network Link interface abstraction may leak technology-specific details, reducing portability.
Vehicle Telemetry interface may not include all required link quality metrics.
evidence_needed: Network coverage maps (cellular, private, satellite) for representative worksites.
Failover switching time measurements from prototype or simulation.
Latency tolerance analysis for teleoperation tasks (steering, tool control).
Cost estimate for dual-path + satellite infrastructure.
Regulatory approval status for satellite uplink in target markets.
Vehicle Telemetry interface specification confirming link quality metrics.
Safety Services SafetyAlert response time SLA.
Evaluation period duration and measurement methodology agreement with customer.
